question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 below, Figure 22 on page 428, and Figure 24 (3E) on page 429.) If the average fuel consumption is 17.5 GPH, how much fuel would you use on the flight between Grand Junction, CO and Durango, CO?
explanation:
DISCUSSION: Complete the flight log by computing the fuel consumption at 17.5 GPH.

HERRM INT.
MANCA INT.
Approach and landing
Total

Time          Fuel (gal.)
:24:00G       7.0
:26:10        7.5
:18:30G       5.5
Total 1:08:40 20.0

G = Given

Answer (A) is incorrect. This amount of fuel would require an average fuel consumption of 15 GPH, not 17.5 GPH. Answer (C) is incorrect. This amount of fuel would require an average fuel consumption of approximately 22 GPH, not 17.5 GPH.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 22 below, Figure 21 on page 427, and Figure 24 (3E) on page 428.) Refer to the FIR except below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight from GJT to DRO.
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-15B Chap 10) DISCUSSION: Complete the flight log for GJT to DRO (Fig. 22). Note that the wind has been interpolated for you from the figures for 12,000 and 18,000 ft. Calculate groundspeed for the cruise portion of the trip using the wind side of the flight computer. Remember to convert wind direction to magnetic direction first; i.e., 230° − 14°E var. = 216° at 8 kt. Then compute ETE for each leg. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 3 min. requires a TAS of 223 kt., not 175 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. An ETE of 58 min. requires a TAS of approximately 293 kt., not 175 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 427, Figure 21A on page 431, Figure 22 on page 428, Figure 23 on page 433, and Figure 24 (3E) on page 429.) What fuel would be consumed on the flight between Grand Junction Co. and Durango, Co. if the average fuel consumption is 15 GPH?
explanation:
Complete the flight log by computing the fuel consumption at 15 GPH.

HERRM INT. :24:00G Fuel (gal.) 6.0
MANCA INT. :26:10 6.5
Approach and landing :18:30G 4.6
Total 1:08:40 17.1

G = Given

Answer (B) is incorrect. This amount of fuel would require an average fuel consumption of 17.5 GPH, not 15 GPH. Answer (C) is incorrect. This amount of fuel would require an average fuel consumption of approximately 22 GPH, not 15 GPH.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 21 on page 427, Figure 21A on page 431, Figure 22 on page 428, Figure 224 on page 432, Figure 23 on page 433, Figure 24 (3E) on page 429, Figure 25 on page 434, and Figure 26 on page 435.) After departing GJT and arriving at Durango Co-La Plata Airport, you are unable to land because of weather. How long can you hold over DRO before departing for return flight to the alternate, Grand Junction Co., Walker Airport? Total usable fuel on board, 68 gallons. Average fuel consumption is 15 GPH. Wind and velocity at 16,000, 2308–16°.
explanation:
To solve this problem, you must calculate times en route and fuel consumption for both trips (GJT-DRO and DRO-GJT), add a 45-minute reserve, and subtract the total fuel used from 68 gal.

First, complete the flight log for each trip (Figs. 22 and 22A).
Calculate groundspeed for the cruise portions of each trip using the wind side of the flight computer. Remember to convert wind direction to magnetic direction; i.e., 230° – 14° E var. = 216° at 8 kt. Then compute the time for each leg and fuel consumption for each trip using the slide rule side of the computer.

GJT to DRO

HERRM INT. Distance X MC X Groundspeed X Time :24:00G Fuel (gal.) 6.0
MANCA INT. 75 151° 172 :26:10 6.5
Approach and Landing X X X :18:30G 4.6
1:08:40 17.1

G = Given

DRO to GJT

MANCA INT. Distance X MC X Groundspeed X Time :14:30G Fuel (gal.) 3.6
HERRM INT. 75 333° 177 :25:21 6.3
JNC 35 331° 177 :11:52 3.0
Approach and Landing X X X :12:00G 3.0
1:03:43 15.9

G = Given

Fuel (gal.)
GJT-DRO 17.1
DRO-GJT 15.9
45-min. reserve 11.3
Total 44.3

With 68 gal. usable fuel, 23.7 gal. (68 – 44.3) would be available to hold at DRO. At 15-GPH fuel consumption, this would be approximately 1 hr. 34 min. of fuel.

Answer (B) is incorrect. To be able to hold for 1 hr. 37 min. would require an average fuel consumption of approximately 14.8 GPH, not 15 GPH. Answer (C) is incorrect. To be able to hold for 1 hr. 42 min. would require an average fuel consumption of approximately 14.5 GPH, not 15 GPH.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 28 below, and Figure 27 on page 438.) What CAS must be used to maintain the filed TAS at the flight planned altitude if the outside air temperature is –5°C?
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-25B Chap 16) DISCUSSION: In the center of the computer side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of –5°C over the altitude of 8,000 ft. (given in block 7 of the flight plan). On the outer scale, find TAS of 155 (from block 4), which is over calibrated airspeed (CAS) on the inner scale of 139 KCAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. Maintaining 134 KCAS would result in a TAS of 150 kt., not 155 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maintaining 142 KCAS would result in a TAS of 158 kt., not 155 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 438 and Legend 36 on page 443.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (AIM Para 5-1-8) DISCUSSION: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash ( / ) and a letter for the equipment code. The bottom portion of Fig. 27 indicates you have a transponder with Mode C and DME, which requires code A. (See Legend 36.) Answer (A) is incorrect. T indicates only a transponder with no encoding capability and no DME. Answer (B) is incorrect. U indicates a transponder with encoding capability but no DME.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 30 on page 440 and Legend 27 on page 442.) Using an average groundspeed of 120 knots, what minimum rate of climb must be maintained to meet the required climb rate (feet per NM) to 4,100 feet as specified on the instrument departure procedure?
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: The instrument departure procedure in Fig. 30 has a note which indicates a minimum climb of 400 ft. per NM to 4,100 ft. To convert this to a rate of climb (fpm), use the formula GS × FT/NM ÷ 60 = FPM = 120 × 400 ÷ 60 = 48,000 ÷ 60 = 800 FPM or use Legend 27 on page 442. Find the 400 ft. per NM column on the left margin and move right to the 120-kt. groundspeed column to determine a rate of climb of 800 fpm. Answer (A) is incorrect. A 400 fpm rate of climb at an average groundspeed of 120 kt. would result in a climb rate of 200 ft. per NM, not 400 ft. per NM. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 500 fpm rate of climb at an average groundspeed of 120 kt. would result in a climb rate of 250 ft. per NM, not 400 ft. per NM.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 27 on page 438, Figure 28 on page 436, Figure 29 on page 439, Figure 30 on page 440, and Figure 31 (3E) on page 441.) (Refer to the FB excerpt below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan. Route of flight: Figs. 27, 28, 29, 30, & 31 (3E) Flight log & MAG VAR GNATS ONE DEPARTURE and Excerpt from AFD FT 3000 6000 9000 OTH 0507 2006+03 2215−05
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-15B Chap 10) DISCUSSION: To determine the estimated time en route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 28. Compute the distance from MERLI intersection to MOURN intersection. First, compute the distance traveled on the OED 15 DME from R-251 (MERLI) to R-333. The number of degrees of the arc is 82° (333 − 251). Use the following general rule to find the distance traveled on the arc: Dist. of arc = # of deg. × DME arc ÷ 60 Dist. of arc = 82 × 15 ÷ 60 = 20.5 NM Next, add the 16 NM on R-333 from the 15 DME arc to MOURN, which is 36.5 (20.5 + 16). Given an average groundspeed of 135 kt., the time is 16 min. 13 sec. For the next three legs, you need to compute the wind triangle on either your flight computer or your electronic computer to determine the groundspeed as given in the table below. Remember that winds are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 28 shows a variation of 20°E. Use the wind at 9,000 ft. because that is closest to the flight planned altitude of 8,000 ft. 220° − 20°E var. = 200° at 15 kt. MERLI INT. Distance MC Wind (Mag) Groundspeed Time :11:00G MOURN INT. X X X X :16:13 RBG VOR 37 287°G 200/15 135G :07:26 OTH VORTAC 19 272°G 200/15 149 :15:12 EUG VORTAC 38 272°G 200/15 170 :20:50 Approach and 59 026°G 200/15 Landing X X X X :10:00G G = Given 1:20:41 Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 10 min. requires a TAS of approximately 200 kt., not 155 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 15 min. requires a TAS of 180 kt., not 155 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 44 on page 446 and Legend 36 on page 450.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: DISCUSSION: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash ( / ) and a letter for an equipment mode. Fig. 44 indicates the airplane has a transponder with Mode C and RNAV, which requires code I. (See Legend 36.) Answer (A) is incorrect. A indicates Mode C transponder and DME but no RNAV. Answer (B) is incorrect. C indicates RNAV and a transponder with no altitude encoding capability.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 44 on page 446.) What CAS must be used to maintain the filed TAS at the flight planned altitude if the outside air temperature is +5°C?
explanation: DISCUSSION: In the center of the slide rule side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of +5°C over the altitude of 12,000 ft. (given in block 7 of the flight plan). On the outer scale, find TAS of 180 kt. (from block 4), which is over calibrated airspeed (CAS) on the inner scale of 147 KCAS. Answer (B) is incorrect. Maintaining 150 KCAS would result in a TAS of 185 kt., not 180 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maintaining 154 KCAS would result in a TAS of 190 kt., not 180 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 44 on page 446, Figure 45 on page 445, Figure 46 on page 447, and Figure 47 (3E) on page 448.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 45. Using the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.
explanation: DISCUSSION: To determine estimated time on route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 45. Using the wind side of your flight computer, determine groundspeeds as given in the table below. Remember that winds are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 45 indicates a variation of 20°E. 200° - 20°E var. = 180° at 35 kt. The distance from BTG VORTAC to PDX is not given. It may be estimated at 10 NM by comparing it to other airway segments of similar length. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 54 min. requires a TAS of 220 kt., not 180 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 7 min. requires a TAS of approximately 165 kt., not 180 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 46 on page 447.) What are the hours of operation (local time) of the ATIS for the Yakima Air Terminal when daylight savings time is in effect?
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: The hours of operation of the ATIS at Yakima Air Terminal are listed on Fig. 46 in the Chart Supplement on the Communications line at ATIS. It operates from 1400Z to 0600Z. Note that the ‡ symbol indicates that, during periods of daylight savings time, effective hours of operation are 1 hr. earlier than those shown (i.e., standard time). For example, the ATIS will begin operation at 6:00 a.m. local time, regardless of whether daylight savings time is in effect. Thus, you should always use the standard conversion, e.g., GMT-8 = 0600-2200 local. Answer (A) is incorrect. You must subtract 8 hr., not 9 hr., from Zulu to convert to local time. Answer (C) is incorrect. You must always use the standard, not daylight, conversion.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 49 on page 449.) With a ground speed of 120 knots, approximately what minimum rate of descent will be required between I-GPO 7 DME fix (ROBOT) and the I-GPO 4 DME fix?
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: The LOC/DME RWY 21 approach chart does not provide descent angle information, so you must calculate the required rate of descent. From the chart, you can see that the 7 DME fix and the 4 DME fix off I-GPO are 3 NM apart. Your ground speed is 120 kt., which will give you 2 NM every minute. Thus, it will take you 1.5 minutes to cover 3 NM. The minimum altitude at ROBOT is 2,300 ft. MSL, and the minimum altitude at the 4 DME fix is 1,100 ft. MSL, a difference of 1,200 ft. Therefore, you must lose 1,200 ft. of altitude in 1.5 minutes, which requires a descent rate of 800 fpm (1,200 ÷ 1.5 = 800). Answer (A) is incorrect. A 1,200-fpm descent rate would be required if your ground speed were 180 kt., not 120 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. A 500-fpm descent rate would be required if your ground speed were 75 kt., not 120 kt.

question_text: Refer to Figure 49 on page 449. What is the usable runway length for landing on runway 21 at PDX?
explanation: In the upper right corner of the airport diagram, there is a notation that says "Rwy 21 ldg 5957." This notation signifies that the runway length available for landing is 5,957 ft.; the remainder of the 7,000 ft. runway length is a displaced threshold. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is not a runway length or runway length available for landing at Portland International (PDX). Answer (B) is incorrect. This is the total length of runway 21, not the runway length available for landing.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 50 on page 452.) What CAS must be used to maintain the filed TAS at the flight planned altitude? (Temperature 0°C.)
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-25B Chap 16) DISCUSSION: In the center of the slide rule side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of 0°C over the altitude of 8,000 ft. (given in block 7 of flight plan). On the outer scale, find TAS of 158 kt. (from block 4), which is over CAS on the inner scale of 140 KCAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. Maintaining 136 KCAS would result in a TAS of 154 kt., not 158 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maintaining 147 KCAS would result in a TAS of 168 kt., not 158 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 50 on page 452 and Legend 36 on page 457.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (AIM Para 5-1-8) DISCUSSION: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash (/) and a letter for the equipment code. Fig. 50 indicates the airplane has a transponder with Mode C and RNAV, which requires Code I. (See Legend 36.) Answer (B) is incorrect. A T indicates only a transponder with no Mode C. Answer (C) is incorrect. A U indicates a transponder with Mode C, but no DME.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 50 on page 452, Figure 51 on page 453, Figure 52 on page 454, and Figure 53 (3E) on page 455.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan. (Refer to the FR excerpt below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.)
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-15B Chap 10) DISCUSSION: To determine the estimated time en route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 51. Using the wind side of your flight computer, determine groundspeeds as given in the table below. Remember that vehicles are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 51 shows a variation of 16°E. Use the wind at 9,000 ft. (which is closest to the planned altitude of 8,000 ft.): 250° − 16°E. var. = 234° at 25 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 43 min. requires a TAS of approximately 205 kt., not 158 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 46 min. requires a TAS of approximately 185 kt., not 158 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 55 on page 456.) Using an average ground speed of 90 knots, what course change rate of descent from 2,400 feet MSL at the 6 DME fix would enable the aircraft to arrive at 2,000 feet MSL at the FAF?
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: The profile view of the IAP chart shows the distance from the 6 DME fix to the FAF to be 3 NM. At 90 kt. (1 1/2 NM per min.), this distance would be covered in 2 min. To descend from 2,400 ft. to 2,000 ft. (400 ft.) in 2 min. would require a descent rate of 200 fpm. Answer (B) is incorrect. This rate would require an average groundspeed of 180 kt., not 90 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. This rate would require an average groundspeed of 270 kt., not 90 kt.

question_text: Refer to Figure 32 on page 460, Figure 33 on page 462, Figure 34 (3E) on page 464, Figure 35 on page 465, Figure 35A on page 466, and Figure 36 on page 466. Refer to the FB excerpt below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan.
explanation: To determine the estimated time en route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 33. As you compare the flight log with the en route and arrival charts (Figs. 34 (3E), 35, and 35A), it will become apparent to you that some alterations of the flight log are necessary. On V573 (Fig. 34 (3E)), there is a bend in the airway (COP) 10 NM southwest of MARKI INT., so an additional leg is required to accurately compute groundspeeds. Also, the check points in the STAR (Figs. 35 and 35A) are not labeled well in the flight log. Having corrected the flight log as shown below, use the wind side of your flight computer to determine groundspeeds as shown. Remember that winds are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 33 shows a variation of 4°E. Use the wind at 9,000 ft. (which is closest to the planned altitude of 8,000 ft.): 220° − 4°E var. = 216° at 40 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 35 min. does not take into account the flight time from the BUJ VORTAC to WEDER INT as explained in the STAR (Figs. 35 and 35A). Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 41 min. requires a TAS of approximately 165 kt., not 180 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 35 on page 465, Figure 35A on page 466, and Figure 37 on page 467.) What is your position relative to the CONNY intersection on the BUJ.BUJ3 transition?
choice_a: Left of the TXK R-272 and approaching the BUJ R-059°.
choice_b: Left of the TXK R-266 and past the BUJ R-065.
choice_c: Right of the TXK R-270 and approaching the BUJ R-245.
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-15B Chap 10) DISCUSSION: Refer to Figs. 35 and 35A and note that this is actually the TXK.BUJ3 transition (to the BUJ.BUJ3 arrival). In Fig. 37, NAV 1 (an RMI) is tuned to 114.9 (BUJ VORTAC) and set to 239°. Since the bar is deflected to the northwest, you are southeast of the BUJ VORTAC. This approaching it (note 270° heading). NAV 2 (an RMI) has the aft needle tuned to 116.3 (TXK VORTAC). The tail of the needle indicates R-270, which is south of R-272 and thus to the left. Answer (B) is incorrect. The R-270 of TXK is right, not left, and you are on, not past, the BUJ R-065. Answer (C) is incorrect. You are on, not right, of the R-270 of TXK and approaching R-244 of LIT, not BUJ.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 36A on page 463.) Under which condition should the missed approach procedure for the VOR/DME RNAV RWY 33 approach initiated?
choice_a: Immediately upon reaching the 5.0 DME from the FAF.
choice_b: When passage of the MAP way point is shown on the ambiguity indicator.
choice_c: After the MDA is reached and 1.8 DME fix from the MAP way point.
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (14 CFR 91.175) DISCUSSION: The missed approach point (MAP) is the point prescribed in each instrument approach procedure at which a missed approach procedure shall be initiated if the required visual reference has not sighted. The MAP on the RNAV RWY 33 approach is arrival at the indicated MAP way point. Passage of the MAP way point is shown on the ambiguity indicator (TO-FROM flag). The FAF is identified as 5.0 DME from the MAP, not vice versa. Answer (A) is incorrect. The FAF is identified as 5.0 DME from the MAP, not vice versa. Assuming a 2.85° glide path, 1.8 DME from the MAP way point is where you would arrive at MDA.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 36A on page 463.) What is the MDA and visibility criteria respectively for the S-33 approach procedure?
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: The MDA and visibility criteria are found in the minimums section of the RNAV RWY 33 approach chart. The straight-in (S-33) MDA and visibility criteria for Category A and B aircraft are 1,240 ft. MSL and 1 SM, respectively (note that the question does not specify the aircraft category). Answer (B) is incorrect. The airplane visibility is not shown to be 1 1/4 SM for any category of aircraft using the straight-in RNAV RWY 33 approach into ADS. Answer (C) is incorrect. These are the minimums for Category A and B aircraft only when the Dallas Love Field altimeter setting is used.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 32 on page 460 and Legend 36 below.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: Answer (C) is correct. (AIM Para 5-1-8) DISCUSSION: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash (/) and a letter for the equipment code. Fig. 32 indicates a transponder with Mode C and RNAV. Thus, Code I is required. (See Legend 36.) Answer (A) is incorrect. A indicates DME and transponder with Mode C but no RNAV. Answer (B) is incorrect. C indicates RNAV and transponder but with no Mode C.

question_text: Refer to Figure 33 below, and Figure 36A on page 463. What is the minimum number of waypoints required for the complete RNAV RWY 33 approach procedure including the IAF's and missed approach procedure?
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (ACL) DISCUSSION: Two waypoints are given for the RNAV RWY 33 approach: ADDIS and the MAP. Answer (A) is incorrect. The MAP is a waypoint (as shown by the box) even though it has no waypoint star. Answer (C) is incorrect. The FAF is not a waypoint; it is a DME fix off of the MAP waypoint.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 38 on page 469.) What number should be placed in block 12?
choice_b: Total fuel to destination, plus a 45 minute reserve.
choice_c: Total fuel to destination, plus a 30 minute reserve.
explanation: DISCUSSION: Block 12 on the flight plan form is intended to record the total amount of usable fuel on board the aircraft in hours of endurance. Answer (B) is incorrect. Block 12 records the total endurance of the aircraft, not the fuel required to the destination plus a 45 minute reserve. Answer (C) is incorrect. Block 12 records the total endurance of the aircraft, not the fuel required to the destination plus a 30 minute reserve.

question_text: Refer to Figure 38 on page 469 and Legend 36 on page 476.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash ( / ) and a letter for the equipment code. Fig. 38 indicates there is a transponder with Mode C and RNAV. Thus, you need Code I. (See Legend 36.) Answer (A) is incorrect. C indicates RNAV and transponder, but with no Mode C. Answer (C) is incorrect. A indicates DME and transponder with Mode C but no RNAV.

question_text: Refer to Figure 38 on page 469.) What CAS must be used to maintain the filed TAS at the flight planned altitude if the outside air temperature is +05°C?
explanation: In the center of the slide rule side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of +5°C over the altitude of 11,000 ft. (from block 7 of the flight plan, Fig. 38). On the outer scale, find TAS of 156 (from block 4), which is over calibrated airspeed on the inner scale of 129 KCAS. Answer (B) is incorrect. Maintaining 133 KCAS would result in a TAS of 161 kt., not 156 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maintaining 139 KCAS would result in a TAS of 168 kt., not 156 kt.

question_text: Refer to Figure 38 on page 469.) What fuel should be entered into block 12 of the flight plan?
choice_b: Total fuel required to destination, plus the fuel to the alternate, plus 45 minutes.
explanation: The total fuel measured in hours should be entered into block 12 of the flight plan. Answer (A) is incorrect. 14 CFR Part 91 requires total fuel measured in hours to be entered into a flight plan, not just the amount of fuel required to reach the destination. Answer (B) is incorrect. 14 CFR Part 91 requires total fuel measured in hours to be entered into a flight plan, not just the amount of fuel required to reach the destination, then fly to the alternate, plus 45 minutes. That is the fuel requirement, not the requirement for flight plan information.

question_text: Refer to Figure 38 on page 469, Figure 39 on page 470, Figure 40 (3E) on page 471, Figure 41 on page 472, and Figure 41A on page 473.) Refer to the FB excerpt below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan.
explanation: To determine the estimated time en route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning leg in Fig. 39. Using the wind of your flight computer, determine groundspeeds as shown in the table below. Remember that winds are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic direction. Fig. 39 shows a variation of 11°E. Use the wind at 12,000 ft. (which is closest to the planned altitude of 11,000 ft.): 210° − 11°E var. = 199° at 42 kt. Wind (Mag.) Groundspeed Time Distance MC BGS VORTAC X X X :06:00G LORAN INT. 42 075° 199/42 176 :14:19 ARI VORTAC 40 076° 199/42 175 :13:43 COTN INT. 63 087° 199/42 167 :22:38 AQN VORTAC 50 075° 199/42 176 :17:03 CREEK INT. 32 040° 199/42 194 :09:54 Approach and Landing X X X X :08:00G 1:31:37 G = Given Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 24 min. requires a TAS of 175 kt., not 156 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 26 min. requires a TAS of 170 kt., not 156 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 41 on page 472, Figure 42 on page 474, and Figure 42A on page 475.) Approaching DFW from Abilene, which frequencies should you expect to use for regional approach control, control tower, and ground control respectively?
explanation: DISCUSSION: Refer to the Communications section of the Chart Supplement in Fig. 42. Since the ACTON two arrival approaches from the west, approach control uses 125.8 or 132.1, tower uses 124.15, and ground uses 121.65 or 121.8. Answer (A) is incorrect. The frequency of 119.05 is for approach control from the east, not west, and 126.55 is for tower from the east, not west. Answer (B) is incorrect. The frequency of 119.05 is for approach control from the east, not west.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 69 on page 480 and Legend 36 on page 482.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (AIM Para 5-1-8) DISCUSSION: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash ( / ) and a letter for the equipment code. Fig. 69 indicates that you have a transponder with Mode C and DME, which requires code A. (See Legend 36.) Answer (B) is incorrect. Code B indicates DME and transponder but no Mode C. Answer (C) is incorrect. Code U indicates only a transponder with Mode C.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 69 on page 480, Figure 70 on page 478, Figure 71 (3E) on page 479, and Figure 72 on page 481.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan. (Refer to the F5 excerpt below, and use the wind entry closest to the flight planned altitude.)
explanation: Answer (B) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-15B Chap 10) DISCUSSION: To determine the estimated time en route to be entered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 70. Using the wind data from your flight computer, determine groundspeeds as shown in the table below. The arrow in Fig. 71 (3E) points to the departure airport. Remember that winds are given in the direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 70 shows a variation of 14°W. Use the wind at 6,000 ft. (which is closest to the planned altitude of 5,000 ft.): 340° + 14°W var. = 354° at 25 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 14 min. requires a TAS of approximately 95 kt., not 128 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. An ETE of 50 min. requires a TAS of 170 kt., not 128 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 69 on page 480.) What CAS should be used to maintain the filed TAS if the outside air temperature is +05°C?
explanation: Answer (A) is correct. (FAA-H-8083-25B Chap 16) DISCUSSION: In the center of the slide rule side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of +5°C over the altitude of 5,000 ft. (from block 4, which is over calibrated airspeed on the inner scale of 119 KCAS. Answer (B) is incorrect. Maintaining 124 KCAS would result in a TAS of 134 kt., not 128 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Maintaining 126 KCAS would result in a TAS of 136 kt., not 128 kt.

question_text: Refer to Figure 70 below, and Figure 71 (3E) on page 479.) Which VORTAC navigation facility along the proposed route of flight could provide you with HIWAS information?
explanation: The Kingston VORTAC (IGN) communication box contains a small square with a white H in the upper right-hand corner indicating availability of HIWAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. The Sparta (SAX) VORTAC communication box has no small square with an H. Answer (B) is incorrect. The Huguenot VORTAC communication box has no small square with an H.

question_text: Refer to Figure 72 on page 481.) How many precision approach procedures are published for Bradley International Airport?
explanation: To determine the number of precision approaches at Bradley International Airport, check the appropriate Chart Supplement, which is reproduced in Fig. 72. At the end of the Chart Supplement, note ILS/DMEs for RWY 6, RWY 33, and RWY 24. Answer (A) is incorrect. There are three, not one, ILSs. Answer (C) is incorrect. There are three, not four, ILSs.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 74 on page 485 and Legend 36 on page 490.) What aircraft equipment code should be entered in block 3 of the flight plan?
explanation: In block 3 of the flight plan, you enter the designation of the aircraft followed by a slash (/) and a letter for the equipment code. Fig. 74 indicates that you have a DME and transponder with Mode C, which requires code A. (See Legend 36.) Answer (A) is incorrect. T indicates only a transponder with no Mode C. Answer (B) is incorrect. U indicates only a transponder with Mode C.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 74 on page 485, Figure 75 on page 484, Figure 76 (3E) on page 486, Figure 77 on page 487, and Figure 78 (3E) on page 488.) Determine the time to be entered in block 10 of the flight plan. (Refer to the ETE excerpt below, and use the wind side of your flight computer, using the wind closest to the flight planned altitude.)
explanation: To determine the estimated time en route to be centered in block 10, you must complete the flight planning log in Fig. 75. Using the wind side of your flight computer, determine groundspeeds as shown in the table below. Remember that winds are given in true direction and must be converted to magnetic. Fig. 75 shows a variation of 18°E. Use the wind at 12,000 ft. (which is closest to the planned altitude of 11,000 ft.): 230° - 18° E var. = 212° at 24 kt. Answer (A) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 15 min. requires a TAS of 195 kt., not 160 kt. Answer (B) is incorrect. An ETE of 1 hr. 20 min. requires a TAS of 175 kt., not 160 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 74 on page 485.) What CAS should be used to obtain the filed TAS at the flight planned altitude if the outside air temperature is 1-0°C?
explanation: In the center of the slide rule side of your flight computer, on the right side, put the air temperature of +5°C over the altitude of 11,000 ft. (from block 7 of the flight plan). On the outer scale, find TAS of 160 (from block 4), which is over calibrated airspeed on the inner scale of 133 KCAS. Answer (A) is incorrect. Obtaining 129 KCAS would result in a TAS of 156 kt., not 133 kt. Answer (C) is incorrect. Obtaining 139 KCAS would result in a TAS of 168 kt., not 133 kt.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 80 on page 489.) What is the TDZE for landing on RWY 27R?
explanation: On the IAP chart, the TDZE is depicted in the airport diagram near the approach end of the landing runway (RWY 27R) as 3,514 ft. MSL. Answer (A) is incorrect. This is the airport elevation, not the TDZE. Answer (C) is incorrect. This is not a relevant altitude at an airport with a 3,649 ft. MSL elevation.

question_text: (Refer to Figure 77 on page 487 and Figure 80 on page 489.) How many initial approach fixes serve the VOR/DME RWY 27R (Billings Logan) approach procedure?
explanation: Initial approach fixes are indicated by the designation "(IAF)" on instrument approach charts (Fig. 80). Note that there is an IAF at the DME arc at R-157 INT and also at the R-040 INT. The Billings VORTAC is also indicated as an IAF, as is MUSTY INT. Answer (A) is incorrect. The VOR/DME RWY 27R approach has four, not three, IAFs. Answer (C) is incorrect. The VOR/DME RWY 27R approach has four, not five, IAFs.
